text: OJ L 272, 25.10.1996, p. 36. This Directive was amended by Regulation (EC) No 1882/2003 of the European Parliament and of the Council (OJ L 284, 31.10.2003, p. 1).

Under Article 7, the managing body of an airport is responsible for providing assistance to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) to ensure they can take their reserved flight. This is provided that notification of the person's specific needs is made to the air carrier, its agent, or the tour operator at least 48 hours before the published departure time. This notification also covers the return flight if both legs are contracted with the same air carrier.

The use of a recognized assistance dog shall be accommodated, provided that notification is given to the air carrier, its agent, or the tour operator in accordance with applicable national rules regarding the carriage of assistance dogs on board aircraft.

If the required 48-hour notification is not provided, the airport managing body must still make all reasonable efforts to provide the necessary assistance to ensure the person can take their reserved flight.

The right to assistance is conditional upon the passenger meeting one of the following timing requirements: (a) presenting themselves for check-in at the time stipulated in writing by the carrier/operator, or if no time is stipulated, at least one hour before departure; or (b) arriving at a designated point within the airport boundary at the time stipulated in writing, or if no time is stipulated, at least two hours before departure.

When a disabled person or person with reduced mobility transits through an airport or is transferred between flights by an air carrier or tour operator, the airport managing body is responsible for providing the assistance necessary to ensure the person can take their reserved flight.

Upon arrival at an airport, the managing body is responsible for providing assistance to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility to ensure they can reach their point of departure from the airport.
Assistance provided to passengers must be tailored, as far as possible, to the specific individual needs of the passenger.
Article 8 establishes the framework for the responsibility for providing assistance at airports.

When organizing assistance for disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility and training relevant personnel, airports and air carriers should refer to European Civil Aviation Conference (ECAC) document 30, Part I, Section 5 and its annexes, specifically the Code of Good Conduct in Ground Handling for Persons with Reduced Mobility.

Airport managing bodies should consider the needs of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when designing new airports and terminals or performing major refurbishments. Similarly, air carriers should account for these needs when designing new or refurbished aircraft.

Directive 95/46/EC regarding the processing of personal data must be strictly enforced to protect the privacy of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility. Information requested must be used solely to fulfill assistance obligations and cannot be used against passengers seeking these services.

All essential information provided to air passengers must be available in alternative formats accessible to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility, and must be provided in at least the same languages as the information available to other passengers.

Passengers must be compensated in accordance with international, Community, and national law if wheelchairs, mobility equipment, or assistive devices are lost or damaged during airport handling or transport on board an aircraft.

Member States must supervise compliance with this Regulation and designate an appropriate body for enforcement. This supervision does not affect the right of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to seek legal redress through national courts.

Passengers who believe this Regulation has been infringed should first notify the airport managing body or the air carrier. If the issue is not resolved, they may file a complaint with the designated Member State enforcement body. Complaints regarding assistance at an airport should be addressed to the enforcement body of the Member State where the airport is located.

Disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) who believe this Regulation has been infringed may report the matter to the managing body of the airport or the relevant air carrier. If satisfaction is not obtained, complaints may be submitted to designated bodies under Article 14(1) or other competent bodies designated by a Member State. Complaints received by a body in one Member State regarding a matter under the responsibility of a body in another Member State shall be forwarded to the appropriate body. Member States must inform disabled persons and PRMs of their rights and the available complaint mechanisms.
Under Article 16, Member States are required to establish rules on penalties for infringements of this Regulation and implement all necessary measures to ensure these rules are enforced.
Penalties for infringements must be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive. Member States must notify the Commission of these penalty provisions and any subsequent amendments.

Under Article 17, the Commission is required to report to the European Parliament and the Council by 1 January 2010 on the operation and effects of this Regulation. This report may include legislative proposals for further implementation or revision of the Regulation.

Under Article 18, this Regulation entered into force on the 20th day following its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union. It became applicable from 26 July 2008, with the exception of Articles 3 and 4, which applied from 26 July 2007. The Regulation is binding in its entirety and directly applicable in all Member States.
This includes the Opinion of the European Parliament of 15 December 2005 and the Council Decision of 9 June 2006.

When designing new airports and terminals, or conducting major refurbishments, airport managing bodies should consider the needs of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs). Similarly, air carriers should consider these needs when designing new or refurbishing aircraft.

To guarantee the privacy of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs), Directive 95/46/EC regarding the processing of personal data must be strictly enforced. Information requested must be used solely to fulfill assistance obligations and cannot be used against passengers seeking these services.

All essential information provided to air passengers must be available in alternative formats accessible to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs). This information must be provided in at least the same languages as the information made available to other passengers.

Passengers must be compensated in accordance with international, Community, and national law if wheelchairs, mobility equipment, or assistive devices are lost or damaged during airport handling or transport on board an aircraft.

Member States are responsible for supervising compliance with this Regulation and must designate an appropriate body for enforcement. This supervision does not limit the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) to seek legal redress through national courts.

Disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) who believe this Regulation has been infringed should first notify the airport managing body or the air carrier. If the issue is not resolved, they may lodge a complaint with the designated enforcement body of the relevant Member State.

Complaints regarding assistance provided at an airport should be addressed to the enforcement body of the Member State where the airport is located. Complaints regarding assistance provided by an air carrier should be addressed to the enforcement body of the Member State that issued the carrier's operating licence.

Member States must establish and apply effective, proportionate, and dissuasive penalties for infringements of this Regulation. These penalties may include ordering the payment of compensation to the affected person.

Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council, dated 5 July 2006, establishes the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) when travelling by air.

Disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs), regardless of whether their condition is caused by disability, age, or other factors, are entitled to air travel opportunities comparable to those of other citizens. This includes the right to free movement, freedom of choice, and non-discrimination.

Disabled persons and PRMs must be accepted for carriage and cannot be refused transport based on their disability or lack of mobility, except for reasons justified by law and safety. Air carriers, their agents, and tour operators should make all reasonable efforts to verify any safety-related reasons that would prevent accommodation before accepting reservations.

Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 does not affect other passenger rights established by Community legislation, specifically Council Directive 90/314/EEC (package travel) and Regulation (EC) No 261/2004 (compensation and assistance). If a single event grants a right to reimbursement or rebooking under both this Regulation and other legislation, the passenger may exercise that right only once, at their discretion.

To ensure air travel opportunities comparable to other citizens, assistance meeting the specific needs of disabled persons and PRMs must be provided at the airport and on board aircraft through the use of necessary staff and equipment. In the interest of social inclusion, this assistance must be provided without additional charge.

Assistance at airports within Member State territories must enable disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to travel from a designated arrival point to the aircraft, and from the aircraft to a designated departure point, including the processes of embarking and disembarking.

Designated points for assistance should be established at main terminal entrances, check-in counter areas, train, light rail, metro and bus stations, taxi ranks, other drop-off points, and airport car parks.

Airport assistance should be organized to prevent interruptions and delays, ensuring high and equivalent standards across the Community while optimizing resources, regardless of the airport or air carrier involved.

The overall responsibility for ensuring high-quality assistance at airports lies with a central body. Because airport managing bodies play a central role in providing services, they are assigned this overall responsibility.

Airport managing bodies may provide assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility directly or contract third parties (including certain operators and air carriers) to supply these services, subject to Community law and public procurement rules.

To avoid disincentives for carrying disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility, assistance should be financed equitably. The most effective funding method is a charge levied on each air carrier using the airport, proportionate to the number of passengers it carries.

Charges levied on air carriers for assistance must be transparent and commensurate with the services provided, ensuring funds are not used for activities other than assistance. In this regard, the provisions on separation of accounts from Council Directive 96/67/EC apply where not in conflict with this Regulation.

When organizing assistance and training personnel, airports and air carriers should refer to European Civil Aviation Conference (ECAC) document 30, Part I, Section 5, and its annexes, specifically the Code of Good Conduct in Ground Handling for Persons with Reduced Mobility (Annex J).

To fund specific requirements, the managing body may levy a transparent, cost-related charge on the air carrier, additional to the charge referred to in Article 8(3). This charge must be established after consultation with the air carrier concerned.

Under Article 10, air carriers must provide the assistance specified in Annex II without additional charge to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility departing from, arriving at, or transiting through an applicable airport, provided the person fulfills the conditions set out in Article 7(1), (2), and (4).

According to Article 11, air carriers and airport managing bodies must: (a) ensure all personnel, including sub-contractors providing direct assistance, know how to meet the needs of persons with various disabilities or mobility impairments; (b) provide disability-equality and disability-awareness training to all airport personnel who deal directly with the public; and (c) ensure all new employees receive disability-related training upon recruitment, with refresher courses provided as appropriate.

Under Article 12, if wheelchairs, other mobility equipment, or assistive devices are lost or damaged while being handled at the airport or transported on board an aircraft, the passenger to whom the equipment belongs shall be compensated in accordance with international, Community, and national law.
Pursuant to Article 13, obligations toward disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility under this Regulation shall not be limited or waived.

Under Article 14, each Member State shall designate a body or bodies responsible for enforcing this Regulation for flights departing from or arriving at airports in its territory. These bodies ensure that the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility are respected, including compliance with quality standards (Article 9(1)) and the satisfactory implementation of Article 8 regarding charges to avoid unfair competition.
Article 15 establishes the framework for the complaint procedure.

When the same event entitles a person to reimbursement or rebooking under multiple legislative acts as well as under this Regulation, the person may exercise that right only once, at their own discretion.

To ensure disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) have air travel opportunities comparable to other citizens, assistance meeting their specific needs must be provided at airports and on board aircraft using necessary staff and equipment. In the interest of social inclusion, this assistance must be provided without additional charge.

Assistance at airports within Member State territories must enable disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to travel from a designated arrival point at the airport to the aircraft, and from the aircraft to a designated departure point, including the processes of embarking and disembarking.

Designated points for assistance should be located at main terminal building entrances, check-in counter areas, transport hubs (including train, light rail, metro, and bus stations), taxi ranks, other drop-off points, and airport car parks.

Assistance must be organized to avoid interruptions and delays, ensuring high and equivalent standards across the Community regardless of the airport or air carrier involved, while making optimal use of resources.

The overall responsibility for ensuring high-quality assistance at airports lies with a central body; specifically, the managing bodies of airports are assigned this responsibility due to their central role in providing airport services.

Airport managing bodies may provide assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility directly or contract the service to third parties, provided they adhere to relevant Community law, including public procurement rules.

Assistance should be financed to spread the burden equitably among all airport users and avoid disincentives for carrying disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility. The most effective funding method is a charge levied on each air carrier proportionate to the number of passengers it carries to or from the airport.

Charges levied on air carriers for assistance must be transparent and commensurate with the services provided, ensuring funds are not used for airport managing body activities unrelated to such assistance. Council Directive 96/67/EC, particularly provisions on the separation of accounts, applies where it does not conflict with this Regulation.
In the event of a conflict between the provisions of this Regulation and those of Directive 96/67/EC, this Regulation shall prevail.

The application of this Regulation to Gibraltar airport is without prejudice to the legal positions of the Kingdom of Spain and the United Kingdom regarding the sovereignty dispute over the territory. Application to Gibraltar airport is suspended until the arrangements in the Joint Declaration of 2 December 1987 enter into operation, at which point the Governments of Spain and the United Kingdom shall inform the Council.

Air carriers, their agents, or tour operators are prohibited from refusing to accept a flight reservation or refusing to embark a person with a disability or reduced mobility at applicable airports, provided the person holds a valid ticket and reservation.

An air carrier, agent, or tour operator may refuse a reservation or embarkation for a person with a disability or reduced mobility if: (a) it is necessary to meet safety requirements established by international, Community, or national law, or requirements set by the authority that issued the air operator's certificate; or (b) the size of the aircraft or its doors makes embarkation or carriage physically impossible.

If a reservation is refused based on safety requirements or physical impossibility, the carrier, agent, or tour operator must make reasonable efforts to propose an acceptable alternative. Persons denied embarkation due to disability or reduced mobility, and their accompanying persons, are entitled to reimbursement or re-routing as provided in Article 8 of Regulation (EC) No 261/2004, provided all safety requirements are met.

Based on the safety requirements established by international, Community, or national law (or the air operator's certificate authority), an air carrier, agent, or tour operator may require that a person with a disability or reduced mobility be accompanied by another person capable of providing the necessary assistance.

Air carriers, their agents, and tour operators must make safety rules and restrictions regarding the carriage of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) publicly available. This includes restrictions on mobility equipment due to aircraft size. Information must be provided in accessible formats and in at least the same languages as the information provided to other passengers.

When an air carrier, its agent, or a tour operator exercises a derogation regarding accessibility rules, they must immediately inform the disabled person or person with reduced mobility of the reasons. Upon request, these reasons must be communicated in writing within five working days.

section: Designation of Airport Arrival and Departure Points
text: Airport managing bodies, in cooperation with airport users, the Airport Users Committee (where applicable), and relevant disability organizations, shall designate arrival and departure points within the airport boundary or under their direct control. These points, located both inside and outside terminal buildings, must allow disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to easily announce their arrival and request assistance.

section: Signage at Arrival and Departure Points
text: Designated arrival and departure points for disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility must be clearly signed and provide basic airport information in accessible formats.

Air carriers, their agents, and tour operators must implement all necessary measures to receive notifications of assistance needs from disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility at all points of sale within the applicable Member States, including via telephone and the Internet.

If a notification of the need for assistance is received at least 48 hours before the published departure time, the air carrier, agent, or tour operator must transmit this information at least 36 hours before departure to: (a) the managing bodies of the departure, arrival, and transit airports, and (b) the operating air carrier, if the reservation was not made with them (or as soon as practicable if the operating carrier is not yet known).
In all cases where the 48-hour notification window is not met, the air carrier, agent, or tour operator must transmit the assistance information as soon as possible.

As soon as possible after a flight departs, the operating air carrier must inform the managing body of the destination airport (if located in a Member State) of the number of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility on the flight requiring assistance and the specific nature of that assistance.
Directive as amended by Regulation (EC) No 1882/2003 (Reference: OJ L 281, 23.11.1995).

Airport managing bodies are responsible for providing assistance and arrangements to enable disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to: communicate arrival and assistance requests at designated points; move to check-in counters; check-in and register baggage; proceed to the aircraft (including emigration, customs, and security); board the aircraft (using lifts, wheelchairs, or other necessary aid); move from the aircraft door to seats; store and retrieve baggage on board; move from seats to the aircraft door; disembark (using lifts, wheelchairs, or other necessary aid); proceed to the baggage hall and retrieve baggage (including immigration and customs); move from the baggage hall to a designated point; reach connecting flights in transit (air and land side); and access toilet facilities.

If requested, an accompanying person assisting a disabled person or person with reduced mobility must be allowed to provide necessary assistance within the airport and during embarking and disembarking.

Ground handling is provided for all necessary mobility equipment. For electric wheelchairs, a 48-hour advance warning is required, and handling is subject to aircraft space limitations and relevant dangerous goods legislation.
Damaged or lost mobility equipment may be temporarily replaced, although the replacement is not necessarily on a like-for-like basis.
Ground handling is provided for recognized assistance dogs where relevant.
Information required for taking flights must be communicated in accessible formats.
Under Annex II, air carriers are responsible for the carriage of recognized assistance dogs in the cabin, subject to national regulations.

Air carriers must allow the transport of up to two pieces of mobility equipment per disabled person or person with reduced mobility. This includes electric wheelchairs, provided that 48 hours' advance warning is given, subject to available space on board and compliance with relevant dangerous goods legislation.
Essential flight information must be provided to passengers in accessible formats.

Air carriers shall make all reasonable efforts to arrange seating that meets the specific needs of individuals with disabilities or reduced mobility, subject to safety requirements and seat availability.
Air carriers must provide assistance in moving to toilet facilities if required by the passenger.

When a disabled person or person with reduced mobility is assisted by an accompanying person, the air carrier will make all reasonable efforts to ensure the accompanying person is seated next to them.
Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council, dated 5 July 2006, establishes the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when travelling by air.

Disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility—regardless of whether the condition is caused by disability, age, or other factors—have the same rights as all other citizens to free movement, freedom of choice, and non-discrimination in air travel.

Disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility must be accepted for carriage and cannot be refused transport based on their disability or lack of mobility, except for reasons justified by safety and prescribed by law.

Prior to accepting reservations, air carriers, their agents, and tour operators should make all reasonable efforts to verify if any safety-justified reasons exist that would prevent a disabled person or person with reduced mobility from being accommodated on the flight.

Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 does not affect other passenger rights established by Community legislation, specifically Council Directive 90/314/EEC regarding package travel and Regulation (EC) No 261/2004 regarding compensation and assistance for denied boarding, cancellations, or long flight delays.

The managing body of an airport is responsible for ensuring that the assistance specified in Annex I is provided to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility without any additional charge to the passenger.

The airport managing body may provide required assistance directly or contract with one or more third parties, provided that quality standards (as referred to in Article 9(1)) are met. Such contracts may be initiated by the managing body or requested by an air carrier, in cooperation with the Airport Users Committee. If a request for contracting is refused, the managing body must provide a written justification.
The managing body of an airport is permitted to levy a specific, non-discriminatory charge on airport users to fund the provision of assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility.

Charges levied for assistance must be reasonable, cost-related, and transparent. These charges are established by the airport managing body in cooperation with airport users (via the Airport Users Committee or another appropriate entity) and are shared among users in proportion to the total number of passengers each carrier transports to and from that airport.

In accordance with current commercial practice, the airport managing body must maintain separate accounts for activities related to the assistance provided to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility, distinct from its other operational accounts.

The airport managing body must provide an audited annual overview of charges received and expenses incurred for assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility. This report must be made available to airport users (via the Airport Users Committee or similar entity) and the relevant enforcement bodies.

Except for airports with fewer than 150,000 annual commercial passenger movements, the managing body must set quality standards for assistance and determine the resource requirements to meet them. This must be done in cooperation with airport users (via the Airport Users Committee) and organizations representing disabled passengers and persons with reduced mobility.

When establishing quality standards, the managing body must fully account for internationally recognized policies and codes of conduct regarding the transport of disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility, specifically the ECAC Code of Good Conduct in Ground Handling for Persons with Reduced Mobility.
The managing body of an airport is required to publish its established quality standards for assistance.

An air carrier and the airport managing body may agree that the managing body will provide assistance of a higher standard than the minimum requirements, or provide additional services beyond those specified in Annex I, for the passengers transported by that specific carrier.
Complaints regarding assistance provided by an air carrier should be addressed to the enforcement body or bodies designated by the Member State that issued the air carrier's operating licence.

Member States must establish and apply effective, proportionate, and dissuasive penalties for infringements of this Regulation, which may include ordering the payment of compensation to the affected person.

To ensure high, equivalent levels of protection and assistance across Member States and harmonized conditions for economic agents in a single market, this Regulation is adopted at the Community level in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty.
In accordance with the principle of proportionality, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary to achieve its objectives.
This Regulation respects fundamental rights and observes the principles recognized by the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.

Arrangements for greater cooperation regarding the use of Gibraltar airport were agreed upon in a joint declaration by the Ministers of Foreign Affairs of Spain and the United Kingdom on 2 December 1987, although these arrangements have yet to enter into operation.

Article 1: This Regulation establishes rules for the protection of and provision of assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRM) traveling by air, to protect them against discrimination and ensure they receive necessary assistance.

The provisions of this Regulation apply to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility using commercial passenger air services departing from, transiting through, or arriving at an airport situated in the territory of a Member State to which the Treaty applies.
Articles 3, 4, and 10 apply to passengers departing from an airport in a third country to an airport in a Member State territory, provided the operating carrier is a Community air carrier.
This Regulation does not affect passenger rights established by Directive 90/314/EEC or Regulation (EC) No 261/2004. In cases of conflict with Directive 96/67/EC, this Regulation shall prevail.

The application of this Regulation to Gibraltar airport is without prejudice to the respective legal positions of the Kingdom of Spain and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland regarding the sovereignty dispute over the territory where the airport is situated.

Passengers with disabilities or reduced mobility, and any accompanying persons, who are denied embarkation based on their disability or reduced mobility, are entitled to reimbursement or re-routing as specified in Article 8 of Regulation (EC) No 261/2004. The option for a return flight or re-routing is subject to all safety requirements being met.

Under specific conditions, an air carrier, its agent, or a tour operator may require a person with a disability or reduced mobility to be accompanied by another person capable of providing the necessary assistance.

Air carriers and their agents must make safety rules regarding the carriage of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility publicly available. This information, including any restrictions based on aircraft size or mobility equipment, must be provided in accessible formats and in at least the same languages as information provided to other passengers.
Tour operators must make safety rules and restrictions available for any flights included in package travel, package holidays, or package tours that they organize, sell, or offer for sale.

When an air carrier, agent, or tour operator exercises a derogation (such as denying embarkation or requiring a companion), they must immediately inform the passenger of the reasons. Upon request, these reasons must be provided in writing within five working days.

The managing body of an airport shall designate points of arrival and departure, both inside and outside terminal buildings, where disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility can easily announce their arrival and request assistance. This process should be done in cooperation with airport users, Airport Users Committees, and relevant disability organizations, taking local conditions into account.

section: Signage and Information at Assistance Points
text: Designated points of arrival and departure for assistance must be clearly signed and provide basic airport information in accessible formats.

Air carriers, their agents, and tour operators must implement all necessary measures to receive notifications of assistance needs from disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility at all points of sale within the applicable Member States, including telephone and internet bookings.

The objectives of this Regulation—ensuring high and equivalent levels of protection and assistance across Member States and harmonizing conditions for economic agents—are better achieved at the Community level than by individual Member States, in accordance with the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality.

Arrangements for greater cooperation regarding the use of Gibraltar airport were agreed upon in a joint declaration by the Ministers of Foreign Affairs of Spain and the United Kingdom on 2 December 1987.
The cooperation arrangements regarding the use of Gibraltar airport have yet to enter into operation.

Article 1(1): This Regulation establishes rules for the protection of and provision of assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) traveling by air, aiming to protect them against discrimination and ensure they receive necessary assistance.

Article 1(2): The provisions of this Regulation apply to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility using or intending to use commercial passenger air services departing from, transiting through, or arriving at an airport situated in the territory of a Member State to which the Treaty applies.

Article 1(3): Articles 3, 4, and 10 also apply to passengers departing from an airport in a third country to an airport in a Member State territory, provided the operating carrier is a Community air carrier.
Article 1(4): This Regulation does not affect the rights of passengers established by Directive 90/314/EEC and Regulation (EC) No 261/2004.

Definition of 'disabled person' or 'person with reduced mobility' (PRM): Any person whose mobility when using transport is reduced due to physical disability (sensory or locomotor, permanent or temporary), intellectual disability or impairment, age, or any other cause of disability, and whose situation requires appropriate attention and adaptation of services to their particular needs.

Definitions of Air Carriers: 
- 'Air carrier': An air transport undertaking with a valid operating licence.
- 'Operating air carrier': An air carrier that performs or intends to perform a flight under a contract with a passenger or on behalf of another person/entity having a contract with that passenger.
- 'Community air carrier': An air carrier with a valid operating licence granted by a Member State in accordance with Council Regulation (EEC) No 2407/92.
Definition of 'tour operator': An organiser or retailer within the meaning of Article 2(2) and (3) of Directive 90/314/EEC, excluding air carriers.

Definitions of Airport Management and Users: 
- 'Managing body of the airport': A body responsible under national legislation for the administration and management of airport infrastructures and the coordination and control of airport operators.
- 'Airport user': Any natural or legal person responsible for the carriage of passengers by air to or from the airport.
- 'Airport Users Committee': A committee of representatives of airport users or organisations representing them.
Definition of 'reservation': The fact that the passenger has a ticket or other proof indicating that the reservation has been accepted and registered by the air carrier or tour operator.

Definitions of Airport Infrastructure: 
- 'Airport': Any area of land specially adapted for the landing, taking-off, and manoeuvres of aircraft, including ancillary installations for aircraft traffic and commercial air services.
- 'Airport car park': A car park within airport boundaries or under the direct control of the managing body that directly serves passengers using that airport.

Definition of 'commercial passenger air service': A passenger air transport service operated by an air carrier through scheduled or non-scheduled flights offered to the general public for valuable consideration, whether independently or as part of a package.

Airports with an annual traffic of 150,000 or more commercial passenger movements must establish quality standards and resource requirements for the assistance specified in Annex I. This process must be conducted in cooperation with airport users, the Airport Users Committee (if applicable), and organizations representing disabled passengers and persons with reduced mobility (PRM). These standards must account for internationally recognized policies, such as the ECAC Code of Good Conduct in Ground Handling for Persons with Reduced Mobility, and must be published by the airport managing body. Furthermore, air carriers and airport managing bodies may agree to provide assistance of a higher standard or additional services beyond those specified in Annex I.

To fund higher standards or additional assistance services, the airport managing body may levy a charge on the air carrier. This charge must be transparent, cost-related, and established after consultation with the concerned air carrier.

Air carriers must provide the assistance specified in Annex II without additional charge to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility (PRM) departing from, arriving at, or transiting through an applicable airport, provided the passenger fulfills the conditions set out in Article 7(1), (2), and (4).

Air carriers and airport managing bodies are required to: (a) ensure all personnel, including sub-contractors providing direct assistance, know how to meet the needs of persons with various disabilities or mobility impairments; (b) provide disability-equality and disability-awareness training to all airport personnel who deal directly with the traveling public; and (c) ensure all new employees receive disability-related training upon recruitment, with refresher courses provided as appropriate.

Passengers shall be compensated in accordance with international, Community, and national law if their wheelchairs, other mobility equipment, or assistive devices are lost or damaged while being handled at the airport or transported on board an aircraft.

This Regulation does not affect other passenger rights established by Community legislation, specifically Council Directive 90/314/EEC regarding package travel and Regulation (EC) No 261/2004 regarding compensation and assistance for denied boarding, cancellations, or long flight delays. If a single event entitles a passenger to reimbursement or rebooking under both this Regulation and the aforementioned acts, the passenger may exercise that right only once, at their own discretion.

To ensure disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) have air travel opportunities comparable to other citizens, necessary staff and equipment must be provided to meet their specific needs at airports and on board aircraft. In the interest of social inclusion, this assistance must be provided without additional charge.

section: Scope and Designated Points of Assistance
text: Assistance at airports within Member State territories must enable disabled persons and PRMs to travel from a designated point of arrival to the aircraft, and from the aircraft to a designated point of departure, including embarking and disembarking. Designated points include main terminal entrances, check-in areas, transport hubs (train, light rail, metro, bus stations), taxi ranks, drop-off points, and airport car parks.

Airport assistance should be organized to prevent interruptions and delays, ensuring high and equivalent standards across the Community regardless of the airport or air carrier involved, while optimizing the use of resources.

The overall responsibility for ensuring high-quality assistance at airports rests with a central body. Because airport managing bodies are central to the provision of services throughout the airport, they are assigned this overall responsibility.

Airport managing bodies may provide assistance to disabled persons and PRMs directly. Alternatively, they may contract third-party operators or air carriers to supply this assistance, provided they comply with relevant Community law, including public procurement rules.

To avoid disincentivizing the carriage of disabled persons and PRMs, assistance should be funded equitably among all airport users. The most effective funding method is a charge levied on each air carrier using the airport, proportionate to the number of passengers the carrier transports to or from that airport.

When an air carrier, its agent, or a tour operator receives a notification for assistance at least 48 hours before the published departure time, they must transmit this information to the managing bodies of the departure, arrival, and transit airports, as well as the operating air carrier (if the reservation was not made with them), at least 36 hours before departure. If the operating carrier is unknown at the time of notification, the information must be transmitted as soon as practicable.

In all cases where notification is received less than 48 hours before the published departure time, the air carrier, its agent, or tour operator must transmit the assistance information as soon as possible.

As soon as possible after a flight's departure, the operating air carrier must inform the managing body of the destination airport (if located in a Member State to which the Treaty applies) of the number of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility on board requiring assistance, as well as the specific nature of that assistance.

The managing body of an airport is responsible for ensuring that disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility receive the assistance specified in Annex I to enable them to take their flight, provided that notification of their specific needs was made to the air carrier, its agent, or the tour operator at least 48 hours before the published departure time.
A notification of assistance needs for an outward flight also covers the return flight, provided both flights were contracted with the same air carrier.

The use of a recognized assistance dog shall be accommodated, provided that notification is made to the air carrier, its agent, or the tour operator in accordance with applicable national rules regarding the carriage of assistance dogs on board aircraft.

If no notification of assistance needs is made 48 hours in advance, the airport managing body must still make all reasonable efforts to provide the assistance specified in Annex I to ensure the person concerned is able to take their flight.

The right to assistance is conditional upon the passenger presenting themselves: (a) for check-in at the time stipulated in writing by the air carrier, its agent, or the tour operator, or if no time is stipulated, at least one hour before the published departure time; or (b) at a designated airport boundary point at the time stipulated in writing, or if no time is stipulated, at least two hours before the published departure time.

Charges levied on air carriers for assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility must be transparent and commensurate with the assistance provided. These charges must not be used to finance activities of the managing body other than the provision of such assistance. Council Directive 96/67/EC regarding the separation of accounts applies where it does not conflict with this Regulation.

When organizing the provision of assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility and training personnel, airports and air carriers should refer to European Civil Aviation Conference (ECAC) document 30, Part I, Section 5, and its associated annexes, specifically the Code of Good Conduct in Ground Handling for Persons with Reduced Mobility (Annex J).

section: Accessible Design of Infrastructure and Aircraft
text: Managing bodies of airports should, where possible, consider the needs of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when designing new airports and terminals or conducting major refurbishments. Similarly, air carriers should consider these needs when designing new or refurbished aircraft.

Directive 95/46/EC on the protection of personal data must be strictly enforced to guarantee the privacy of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility. Information requested must be used solely to fulfill assistance obligations and must not be used against passengers seeking these services.

All essential information provided to air passengers must be available in alternative formats accessible to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility. This information must be provided in at least the same languages as the information made available to other passengers.

Passengers must be compensated for wheelchairs, mobility equipment, or assistive devices that are lost or damaged during airport handling or transport on board aircraft, in accordance with international, Community, and national law.

Member States are responsible for supervising and ensuring compliance with this Regulation by designating an appropriate enforcement body. This supervision does not affect the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to seek legal redress through national courts.

Disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility who believe this Regulation has been infringed should first bring the matter to the attention of the airport managing body or the air carrier. If the issue is not resolved, they are free to make a complaint to the body or bodies designated by the relevant Member State.

section: Assistance for Transit and Transfer Passengers
text: The airport managing body is responsible for ensuring that disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility receive the assistance specified in Annex I when transiting through an airport or being transferred between flights by an air carrier or tour operator, ensuring the passenger is able to take their reserved flight.

Upon the arrival of a disabled person or person with reduced mobility at an airport, the managing body is responsible for providing the assistance specified in Annex I to ensure the passenger can reach their point of departure from the airport.
Assistance provided to passengers with disabilities or reduced mobility shall be tailored to the specific needs of the individual passenger as far as possible.
The airport managing body must ensure that the assistance specified in Annex I is provided to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility without any additional charge to the passenger.

The airport managing body may provide the required assistance itself or contract one or more third parties to supply the services, provided that the quality standards referred to in Article 9(1) are maintained.

The managing body may enter into assistance contracts on its own initiative or upon request (including from air carriers), in cooperation with the Airport Users Committee if one exists. If such a request is refused, the managing body must provide a written justification.
The airport managing body may levy a specific charge on airport users on a non-discriminatory basis to fund the provision of assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility.

Charges levied on airport users to fund assistance must be reasonable, cost-related, transparent, and established in cooperation with airport users (via the Airport Users Committee or similar entity). These charges are shared among users in proportion to the total number of passengers each carries to and from the airport.

The airport managing body must maintain separate accounts for activities related to providing assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility, distinct from its other activities, in accordance with current commercial practice.

The airport managing body must provide an audited annual overview of the charges received and expenses incurred for assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility. This report must be available to airport users (via the Airport Users Committee or similar entity) and the relevant enforcement bodies.

Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 prohibits air carriers from refusing reservations or boarding to passengers based on their disability or reduced mobility. The regulation ensures that these passengers receive free-of-charge assistance to enable them to use air transport on an equal footing with other passengers.

Air carriers, their agents, or tour operators may refuse reservation or boarding to a person with reduced mobility or disability under two specific conditions: 1) to meet applicable legal safety requirements (in which case the carrier may require the passenger to be accompanied by a person capable of providing necessary assistance), or 2) if the size of the aircraft or its doors makes boarding or carriage physically impossible. In such cases, the affected person must be informed of the reasons immediately.

When boarding is refused due to safety requirements or aircraft size constraints, reasonable efforts must be made to offer the passenger an acceptable alternative. Passengers denied boarding for these reasons are entitled to reimbursement or re-routing in accordance with Regulation (EC) No 261/2004.

Persons with reduced mobility or disability are entitled to free assistance in airports (during departure, arrival, and transit) and on board aircraft, including the transport of mobility equipment and the carriage of guide dogs for the blind. Airport managing bodies may fund these services by levying a specific charge on airport users.
EU and European Free Trade Association countries are required to establish penalties for infringements of Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 and must set up independent bodies to handle complaints.
Regulation (EC) No 261/2004 establishes EU-wide rules regarding compensation and assistance for passengers in the event of denied boarding, flight cancellations, or long delays.
In 2012, the European Commission published guidelines to assist in the interpretation of Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006.
The 2012 European Commission guidelines were developed to address practical problems and uncertainties remaining for both air carriers and passengers with disabilities or reduced mobility.

If an air carrier, its agent, or a tour operator refuses a reservation based on specific grounds (such as safety or aircraft limitations), they must make reasonable efforts to propose an acceptable alternative to the person concerned.

Disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility who are denied embarkation due to their disability or reduced mobility, including any accompanying persons, are entitled to reimbursement or re-routing as specified in Article 8 of Regulation (EC) No 261/2004. The option for a return flight or re-routing is subject to all safety requirements being met.

Under specific safety conditions, an air carrier, its agent, or a tour operator may require that a disabled person or person with reduced mobility be accompanied by another person capable of providing the necessary assistance.

section: Publication of Safety Rules and Restrictions
text: Air carriers and their agents must publicly provide safety rules and restrictions regarding the carriage of disabled persons, persons with reduced mobility, and mobility equipment (including restrictions due to aircraft size). This information must be available in accessible formats and in the same languages as information provided to other passengers. Tour operators must provide these rules for any package travel, holidays, or tours they organize or sell.

When an air carrier, agent, or tour operator exercises a derogation (exception) regarding the carriage of disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility, they must inform the person immediately of the reasons. Upon request, these reasons must be provided in writing within five working days.

The managing body of an airport shall designate points of arrival and departure, both inside and outside terminal buildings, where disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility can easily announce their arrival and request assistance. This designation should be done in cooperation with airport users, Airport Users Committees, and relevant organizations representing disabled persons.

section: Signage and Information at Assistance Points
text: Designated airport arrival and departure points for assistance must be clearly signed and provide basic airport information in accessible formats.

Complaints regarding assistance provided at an airport should be addressed to the enforcement body designated by the Member State where the airport is located. Complaints regarding assistance provided by an air carrier should be addressed to the enforcement body designated by the Member State that issued the carrier's operating licence.

This Regulation is adopted at the Community level to ensure high and equivalent levels of protection and assistance across Member States and to harmonize conditions for economic agents in a single market, adhering to the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty.
The cooperation arrangements regarding Gibraltar airport have not yet entered into operation.

This Regulation establishes rules for the protection of and provision of assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRM) traveling by air, aiming to protect them against discrimination and ensure they receive necessary assistance.

The provisions of this Regulation apply to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility using or intending to use commercial passenger air services when departing from, transiting through, or arriving at an airport situated in the territory of a Member State to which the Treaty applies.

Articles 3, 4, and 10 of this Regulation also apply to passengers departing from an airport in a third country to an airport in a Member State, provided the operating carrier is a Community air carrier.
This Regulation does not affect the rights of passengers established by Directive 90/314/EEC and Regulation (EC) No 261/2004.

A 'disabled person' or 'person with reduced mobility' is defined as any person whose mobility when using transport is reduced due to physical disability (sensory or locomotor, permanent or temporary), intellectual disability or impairment, age, or any other cause of disability, and whose situation requires appropriate attention and adaptation of services to meet their particular needs.

The regulation defines three types of air carriers: (a) 'air carrier' refers to an air transport undertaking with a valid operating licence; (b) 'operating air carrier' is an air carrier that performs or intends to perform a flight under a contract with a passenger or on behalf of another party; (c) 'Community air carrier' is an air carrier with a valid operating licence granted by a Member State in accordance with Council Regulation (EEC) No 2407/92.
A 'tour operator' is defined as an organiser or retailer within the meaning of Article 2(2) and (3) of Directive 90/314/EEC, excluding air carriers.

Definitions regarding airport administration include: (a) 'managing body of the airport' is a body responsible for the administration and management of airport infrastructures and the coordination of operators; (b) 'airport user' is any natural or legal person responsible for the carriage of passengers by air to or from the airport; (c) 'Airport Users Committee' is a committee representing airport users or their organisations.

Definitions for airport operations include: (a) 'reservation' is proof (such as a ticket) that a reservation has been accepted and registered by the air carrier or tour operator; (b) 'airport' is land adapted for aircraft landing, take-off, and manoeuvres, including ancillary installations; (c) 'airport car park' is a car park within airport boundaries or under the managing body's control that serves passengers; (d) 'commercial passenger air service' is a scheduled or non-scheduled passenger transport service offered to the general public for valuable consideration.
In instances where the provisions of this Regulation conflict with those of Directive 96/67/EC, this Regulation shall prevail.

The application of this Regulation to Gibraltar airport is subject to the legal positions of the Kingdom of Spain and the United Kingdom regarding sovereignty disputes. Application is suspended until the arrangements in the Joint Declaration of 2 December 1987 enter into operation, with both governments to inform the Council of the start date.

Under Article 3, air carriers, their agents, or tour operators are prohibited from refusing to accept a flight reservation or refusing to embark a disabled person or person with reduced mobility on the grounds of their disability, provided the person has a valid ticket and reservation for a flight departing from or arriving at an airport covered by this Regulation.

Under Article 4, an air carrier, agent, or tour operator may refuse a reservation or embarkation for a disabled person or person with reduced mobility only if: (a) it is necessary to meet safety requirements established by international, Community, national law, or the authority that issued the air operator's certificate; or (b) the size of the aircraft or its doors makes embarkation or carriage physically impossible.
Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 entered into force on 15 August 2006.

section: Definition of Disabled Person or Person with Reduced Mobility
text: A disabled person or person with reduced mobility is defined as any person whose mobility when using transport is reduced due to physical disability (sensory or affecting mobility, whether permanent or temporary), intellectual disability or impairment, or any other cause of disability or age, who requires appropriate attention and adaptation of the service to meet their particular needs.

Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 5 July 2006 concerns the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when travelling by air (OJ L 204, 26.7.2006, pp. 1-9).

Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 is related to Regulation (EC) No 261/2004 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 February 2004, which establishes common rules on compensation and assistance to passengers in the event of denied boarding, cancellation, or long flight delays.

Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council, dated 5 July 2006, concerns the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when travelling by air. It is identified by CELEX 32006R1107 and was published in OJ L 204/1 on 26 July 2006.

The regulation was adopted by the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union, having regard to the Treaty establishing the European Community (specifically Article 80(2)), a proposal from the Commission, the opinion of the European Economic and Social Committee, and consultation with the Committee of the Regions.
The regulation is based on the principle that the single market for air services should benefit all citizens in general.

Disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility, regardless of whether the condition is caused by disability, age, or any other factor, have the right to air travel opportunities comparable to other citizens, including the rights to free movement, freedom of choice, and non-discrimination.

Disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility must be accepted for carriage and cannot be refused transport on the grounds of their disability or lack of mobility, except for reasons justified by safety and prescribed by law.

Before accepting reservations from disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility, air carriers, their agents, and tour operators should make all reasonable efforts to verify if there are safety-justified reasons that would prevent the person from being accommodated on the flight.

When an air carrier, agent, or tour operator receives a notification for assistance at least 48 hours before the published departure time, they must transmit this information to the managing bodies of the departure, arrival, and transit airports, and to the operating air carrier (if the reservation was not made with them), at least 36 hours before departure. If the operating carrier is unknown at the time of notification, the information must be transmitted as soon as practicable.

In all cases where notification of the need for assistance is received less than 48 hours before the published departure time, the air carrier, agent, or tour operator must transmit the relevant information as soon as possible.

As soon as possible after a flight departs, the operating air carrier must inform the managing body of the destination airport (if located in a Member State to which the Treaty applies) of the number of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility on board who require assistance, as well as the specific nature of that assistance.

The managing body of an airport is responsible for ensuring the provision of assistance to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility so they can take their flight, provided that notification of their specific needs was made to the air carrier, agent, or tour operator at least 48 hours before the published departure time. This notification also covers the return flight if both the outward and return flights were contracted with the same air carrier.

The use of a recognized assistance dog shall be accommodated, provided that notification is made to the air carrier, agent, or tour operator in accordance with applicable national rules governing the carriage of assistance dogs on board aircraft.

If no notification of assistance needs is made at least 48 hours before departure, the airport managing body must still make all reasonable efforts to provide the necessary assistance to ensure the person can take their flight.

The right to assistance is conditional upon the person presenting themselves: (a) for check-in at the time stipulated in writing by the air carrier, agent, or tour operator, or if no time is stipulated, at least one hour before the published departure time; or (b) at a designated point within the airport boundary at the time stipulated in writing, or if no time is stipulated, at least two hours before the published departure time.

A 'disabled person' or 'person with reduced mobility' is defined as any person whose mobility when using transport is reduced due to age, intellectual disability or impairment, or any other cause of disability, including permanent or temporary physical disabilities (sensory or locomotor). Such persons require appropriate attention and the adaptation of services to meet their particular needs.

The following definitions apply to air transport providers:
- 'Air carrier': An air transport undertaking holding a valid operating licence.
- 'Operating air carrier': An air carrier that performs, or intends to perform, a flight under a contract with a passenger or on behalf of another person (legal or natural) who has a contract with that passenger.
- 'Community air carrier': An air carrier with a valid operating licence granted by a Member State in accordance with Council Regulation (EEC) No 2407/92.

The following definitions apply to airport administration and associated entities:
- 'Tour operator': An organiser or retailer (excluding air carriers) as defined in Directive 90/314/EEC.
- 'Managing body of the airport': A body responsible under national legislation for the administration and management of airport infrastructures, and the coordination and control of operators within an airport or airport system.
- 'Airport user': Any natural or legal person responsible for the carriage of passengers by air to or from the airport.
- 'Airport Users Committee': A committee composed of representatives of airport users or organisations representing them.

The following operational definitions apply:
- 'Reservation': Proof (such as a ticket) indicating that a reservation has been accepted and registered by the air carrier or tour operator.
- 'Airport': Land specially adapted for aircraft landing, take-off, and manoeuvres, including ancillary installations for aircraft traffic and commercial air services.
- 'Airport car park': A car park within airport boundaries or under the direct control of the managing body that serves passengers.
- 'Commercial passenger air service': A passenger air transport service operated by an air carrier via scheduled or non-scheduled flights offered to the general public for valuable consideration.

The application of this Regulation to Gibraltar airport is suspended until the arrangements specified in the Joint Declaration made by the Foreign Ministers of Spain and the United Kingdom on 2 December 1987 enter into operation. The governments of Spain and the United Kingdom shall notify the Council of the date of entry into operation.

Under Article 3, air carriers, their agents, or tour operators are prohibited from refusing to accept a flight reservation or embark a disabled person or person with reduced mobility on the grounds of their disability, provided the person possesses a valid ticket and reservation.

section: Derogations and Special Conditions for Refusal
text:
Notwithstanding the general prohibition on refusal of carriage, an air carrier, agent, or tour operator may refuse a reservation or embarkation for a disabled person or person with reduced mobility if:
(a) it is necessary to meet safety requirements established by international, Community, or national law, or requirements set by the authority that issued the air operator's certificate;
(b) the size of the aircraft or its doors makes embarkation or carriage physically impossible.
In such cases, the carrier, agent, or tour operator must make reasonable efforts to propose an acceptable alternative to the person concerned.

When a disabled person or person with reduced mobility transits through an airport or is transferred between flights by an air carrier or tour operator, the airport managing body is responsible for ensuring the assistance specified in Annex I is provided so the person can take their reserved flight.

Upon the arrival of a disabled person or person with reduced mobility at an airport, the managing body is responsible for ensuring the assistance specified in Annex I is provided so the person can reach their point of departure from the airport.
Assistance provided to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility shall be tailored, as far as possible, to the specific needs of the individual passenger.
The managing body of an airport must ensure that the assistance specified in Annex I is provided to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility without any additional charge to the passenger.

The airport managing body may provide assistance itself or contract with third parties to supply it, provided quality standards (Article 9(1)) are met. Contracts may be initiated by the managing body or requested by airport users (including air carriers) via the Airport Users Committee; any refusal of such requests must be justified in writing.
To fund the provision of assistance, the airport managing body may levy a specific charge on airport users on a non-discriminatory basis.

The airport managing body must maintain separate accounts for activities related to the assistance of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility, distinct from its other activities, in accordance with current commercial practice.

section: Transparency and Auditing of Assistance Funds
text: The airport managing body must provide an audited annual overview of charges received and expenses incurred for assistance to airport users (via the Airport Users Committee or similar entity) and to the relevant enforcement bodies.
Obligations toward disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility pursuant to this Regulation shall not be limited or waived.

Each Member State must designate one or more bodies responsible for enforcing this Regulation for flights arriving at or departing from airports within its territory. These bodies are tasked with ensuring that the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility are respected, including compliance with the quality standards specified in Article 9(1). Member States must inform the Commission of the designated bodies.

Member States may provide that the designated enforcement bodies also ensure the satisfactory implementation of Article 8, particularly regarding provisions on charges to avoid unfair competition. Alternatively, a specific body may be designated for this purpose.

A disabled person or person with reduced mobility who believes this Regulation has been infringed may bring the matter to the attention of the airport's managing body or the air carrier concerned. If satisfaction is not obtained, complaints may be made to the designated enforcement body under Article 14(1) or any other competent body designated by the Member State.

If a designated body in one Member State receives a complaint regarding a matter that falls under the responsibility of a designated body in another Member State, it must forward the complaint to the appropriate body in that other Member State.

Member States must take measures to inform disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility of their rights under this Regulation and the possibility of filing complaints with the designated enforcement bodies.
Member States shall establish rules on penalties applicable to infringements of this Regulation and take all necessary measures to ensure those rules are implemented.
Penalties for infringements must be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive. Member States must notify the Commission of these penalty provisions and any subsequent amendments without delay.

The Commission shall report to the European Parliament and the Council by 1 January 2010 on the operation and effects of this Regulation. This report shall include legislative proposals for further implementation or revision of the Regulation where necessary.

section: Article 18: Entry into Force and Applicability
text: This Regulation entered into force on the 20th day following its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union. It became applicable on 26 July 2008, with the exception of Articles 3 and 4, which became applicable on 26 July 2007. The Regulation is binding in its entirety and directly applicable in all Member States.

Except for airports with fewer than 150,000 annual commercial passenger movements, the managing body must establish quality standards for assistance (as specified in Annex I) and determine the resources required to meet them. This process must be conducted in cooperation with airport users, the Airport Users Committee (where applicable), and organizations representing disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRM). These standards must account for internationally recognized policies, such as the ECAC Code of Good Conduct in Ground Handling for Persons with Reduced Mobility, and must be published by the managing body. Additionally, air carriers and airport managing bodies may agree on higher standards or additional services beyond those specified in Annex I.

To fund higher standards or additional services agreed upon between an air carrier and the airport managing body, the managing body may levy a transparent, cost-related charge on the air carrier, established after consultation with the carrier concerned.

Under Article 10, air carriers must provide the assistance specified in Annex II free of charge to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility (PRM) departing from, arriving at, or transiting through an airport covered by this Regulation, provided the passenger meets the conditions set out in Article 7(1), (2), and (4).

According to Article 11, air carriers and airport managing bodies must: (a) ensure all personnel, including sub-contractors providing direct assistance, are knowledgeable about meeting the needs of persons with various disabilities or mobility impairments; (b) provide disability-equality and disability-awareness training to all airport personnel who interact directly with the traveling public; and (c) ensure all new employees receive disability-related training upon recruitment, with refresher courses provided as appropriate.

Under Article 12, if wheelchairs, mobility equipment, or assistive devices are lost or damaged while being handled at the airport or transported on board an aircraft, the passenger to whom the equipment belongs shall be compensated in accordance with international, Community, and national law.
Pursuant to Article 13, obligations toward disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility under this Regulation shall not be limited or waived.

Under Article 14, each Member State must designate one or more bodies responsible for enforcing this Regulation for flights departing from or arriving at airports within its territory. These bodies ensure that the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility are respected, including compliance with quality standards. Member States must inform the Commission of these designated bodies and may ensure these bodies also oversee the implementation of Article 8 regarding charges to prevent unfair competition.

According to Article 15, disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility who believe this Regulation has been infringed may report the matter to the airport managing body or the air carrier. If the issue is not resolved, complaints may be submitted to the designated enforcement bodies or other competent national bodies. Complaints received by a body in one Member State regarding a matter under the responsibility of another Member State shall be forwarded to the appropriate body in that state. Member States must inform passengers of their rights and the available complaint procedures.
Under Article 16, Member States are required to establish rules on penalties applicable to infringements of this Regulation and must take all necessary measures to ensure those rules are implemented.
As per Article 16, penalties for infringements must be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive. Member States must notify the Commission of these penalty provisions and any subsequent amendments.

Under Article 17, the Commission is required to report to the European Parliament and the Council by January 1, 2010, on the operation and effects of this Regulation. This report may include legislative proposals to further detail or revise the Regulation.

According to Article 18, this Regulation entered into force on the 20th day following its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union. It became applicable on July 26, 2008, with the exception of Articles 3 and 4, which applied from July 26, 2007. The Regulation is binding in its entirety and directly applicable in all Member States.
